project.name: OneBasePlate
project.goal: Reduce grocery stress, separate cooking events, takeaway reliance, and food waste in households with overlapping but conflicting food preferences, including ARFID-style preparation constraints, picky children, and babies requiring texture-adapted food.
project.northStar: One base meal, multiple household-specific assemblies, with a merged grocery list and low-decision rescue mode.
primarySuccessMetrics: Reduce number of separate cooking events per day
Increase percentage of dinners assembled from one shared base
Reduce food waste from unused ingredients
Reduce time spent deciding what to cook
nonGoals: Building a massive public recipe marketplace in v1
Advanced nutrition coaching or medical advice
Live grocery delivery integrations in v1
Fully automated AI meal generation without explicit household constraints

anthropicLongRunningAgentAlignment.intent: Structure the repo and work plan so a long-running coding agent can make safe incremental progress across sessions.
principles: Use a dedicated initializer/setup phase before feature implementation begins.
Represent requirements as a structured JSON feature list with pass/fail status rather than freeform notes.
Work on one feature at a time and leave the codebase in a clean, mergeable state after each session.
Persist progress in a progress log plus git history so future sessions can recover context quickly.
Start each session by reading the feature list, recent progress, and repository state.
Run end-to-end verification before marking any feature as passing.
Favor deterministic fixtures and explicit acceptance steps over vague goals.
When a feature references uiSpec screens, read those screen definitions before implementation and verification.
sessionLoop: Run init.sh and verify the app boots successfully.
Read agent-progress.md and recent git log entries.
Read PRD.json and select the highest-priority feature with passes=false and no blocked dependencies.
Read any uiSpec screen definitions referenced by the selected feature before making changes.
Implement only that feature and any strictly necessary enabling refactor.
Run unit tests and end-to-end checks relevant to the feature.
Update only the affected passes fields and append a short progress summary.
Commit the work with a descriptive message.

personas.name: Constraint-led adult eater
personas.description: Adult with ARFID-like or highly specific preparation constraints.
needs: Food prepared in an acceptable way

personas.name: Picky toddler
personas.description: Young child with a small set of accepted foods and fast-changing tolerance.
needs: At least one safe food on the plate

personas.name: Weaning baby
personas.description: Baby requiring soft, mashable, or finger-safe texture adaptations.
needs: Simple substitutions when base meal is unsuitable

personas.name: Busy home-working adult
personas.description: Adult with limited time who wants low-decision household planning.
needs: Predictable system that reduces mental load

coreConcepts.baseMeal: A flexible meal made of components that can be assembled differently for each household member.
coreConcepts.assemblyVariant: A per-person presentation of the base meal that respects preferences, preparation constraints, and texture needs.
coreConcepts.safeFood: A reliably accepted ingredient or meal component for a person.
coreConcepts.rescueMeal: A low-effort meal generated for high-stress or low-time situations using pantry/freezer staples first.
coreConcepts.overlapScore: A score showing how many household members can eat a given ingredient or base meal with acceptable modifications.
coreConcepts.multiProteinBaseMeal: A base meal with one shared structure and multiple selectable protein paths so household members can receive different proteins without splitting the meal into separate dinners.
coreConcepts.recipeLink: An optional external or internal link attached to a base meal to support repeatable preparation, shortcuts, or meal-kit references.
coreConcepts.componentRecipeRef: Optional per-component preparation pointer (internal base meal, imported recipe row, external URL, or freeform note). Stored on MealComponent.recipeRefs for defaults and copied or overridden per night on DayPlan.componentRecipeOverrides.
coreConcepts.weeklyThemeAnchor: Optional soft weekday theme (label, tags, structure hints, optional meal ids) on Household.weeklyAnchors; ranking uses it only as a late tie-breaker so themes never override strong household fit signals.
coreConcepts.rescueMode: A planning mode for nights when time, energy, or household tolerance is low, prioritizing the fastest acceptable dinner using staples first.

entities.name: BaseMeal
fields: recipeRefs[] (RecipeRef, optional; whole-meal / assembly / shortcut recipe references)

entities.name: DayPlan
fields: componentRecipeOverrides[] (ComponentRecipeRef, optional; plan-night only, does not mutate BaseMeal defaults)

entities.name: Household
fields: recipes[] (Recipe library rows, optional until normalized)

entities.name: MealComponent
fields: id (optional; stable; assigned on load/save if missing)

canonicalStorageFormat.rule: Ingredient names must be stored in lowercase.

normalizationRules: Compare normalized names to detect duplicates

uiDisplayFormatting.rule: UI displays ingredient names in sentence case while preserving lowercase canonical storage.

importHandling: Normalize imported ingredient name before lookup
Attempt to match an existing ingredient using the normalized value
If no match exists, create a new ingredient using the normalized lowercase name

existingIngredientMigration.goal: Apply the ingredient naming standard to legacy ingredient records so old and newly imported data follow one canonical format.
duplicateDetectionExamples: Red Onion and red onion should resolve to one ingredient
Soy Sauce and soy sauce should resolve to one ingredient
Garlic. and garlic should resolve to one ingredient
mergeBehavior: Keep one surviving ingredient record per normalized name
Reassign all related references from duplicate ingredient ids to the surviving ingredient id
Remove or archive duplicate ingredient records only after successful reassignment
Preserve useful metadata from duplicates when one record is more complete
dataIntegrityRequirements: No meal, recipe, tag, catalog link, or ingredient reference may remain pointed at a removed duplicate id
Migration updates must be idempotent where possible
Prefer merge and reassignment over destructive deletion
existingIngredientMigration.uiRequirement: After migration, ingredient names remain stored in lowercase and are displayed in sentence case in the UI.

ingredientNamingStandard.futureCatalogCompatibility: Normalized lowercase ingredient name is the matching key across catalog ingredients, imported ingredients, and user-created ingredients.
acceptanceCriteria: Existing mixed-case ingredients are normalized to lowercase storage
Duplicate ingredients created only by casing, spacing, or trailing punctuation differences are merged
All related records continue to work after migration with no orphaned references
UI continues to show sentence-case ingredient names while storage remains lowercase
doNot: Store multiple casing variations of the same ingredient
Create separate ingredients that differ only by capitalization
Leave orphaned references after duplicate merges
Change user-facing ingredient behavior except cleaner display consistency

suggestedStack.storage: Local-first JSON or IndexedDB/localStorage for v1

architectureNotes: Prefer local-first storage for rapid MVP delivery and privacy.
Keep the rule engine explicit and deterministic before adding AI generation.
Ship fixtures for 3-5 representative households to support repeatable development and testing.
Treat the PRD feature list as the source of truth for incremental implementation.
Model multi-protein meals explicitly so the planner can treat one shared structure as a single dinner with different protein paths.
Keep recipe links and preparation notes on BaseMeal so service shortcuts and household-specific tips can persist.
Implement the weekly planner as a visual mini-calendar with meal cards before adding advanced learning behaviors.
Treat the companion UI spec as the screen-level source of truth for layout, readability, and interaction polish.
Adopt a shared styling foundation before further UX polish so already-implemented screens can be refactored without reworking planner logic later.
Treat responsive mobile layouts as a core requirement for every user-facing screen rather than a final cleanup pass.

features.description: Repository initializer creates bootstrap artifacts for long-running agent work
steps: Create project scaffold and package metadata
Add init.sh to install dependencies and start the local app
Add agent-progress.md with initial status and conventions
Add PRD.json to repo root as the structured source of truth
Add sample fixtures for at least one representative household

features.description: User can create a household with multiple members and role types
steps: Navigate to household setup
Add at least four members with adult, toddler, and baby roles
Save the household profile
Re-open the household
Verify all members persist correctly and the Household Setup screen still feels guided rather than form-heavy.

features.description: User can record safe foods, hard-no foods, and preparation rules for each household member
steps: Open a member profile
Add safe foods, hard-no foods, and preparation rules
Save the profile
Re-open the profile
Verify all constraints are preserved exactly and quick edit entry points remain available on Household Setup.

features.description: User can define pantry, fridge, freezer, and staple ingredients
steps: Navigate to household ingredients
Add ingredients across pantry, fridge, and freezer categories
Tag ingredients with properties such as quick, mashable, or rescue
Normalize ingredient names on create and edit by lowercasing, trimming whitespace, collapsing duplicate spaces, and removing trailing punctuation before saving.
Save the ingredient set
Verify ingredients are available to the planner

features.description: User can create or select a base meal made from reusable components
steps: Navigate to base meals
Create a meal from components such as protein, carb, and veg
Set time and rescue eligibility metadata
Verify the Base Meal Editor supports structure type, protein options, carb options, and rescue metadata without a dense form layout.
Save the base meal
Verify it appears in meal selection and can be reopened from the Base Meal Editor.

features.description: Planner generates one base meal with per-person assembly variants
steps: Open the planner for a household with saved constraints
Select or generate a base meal
View assembly instructions for each member
Verify meal cards and meal detail views present the shared base meal and per-person variants clearly.
Verify each member receives a compatible version or an explicit fallback
Verify the plan clearly identifies shared versus extra preparation and reads as one base meal with multiple assemblies.

features.description: Planner adapts baby servings into texture-safe versions for a no-teeth weaning stage
steps: Open a generated meal that includes a baby household member
Inspect the baby assembly instructions
Verify the output transforms unsuitable textures into soft, mashable, or finger-safe guidance
Verify obviously unsafe suggestions are excluded
Verify the baby variant stays tied to the same base meal where possible

features.description: Planner ensures toddler plate includes at least one safe food
steps: Generate a meal for a household with a toddler profile
Inspect the toddler assembly variant
Verify at least one marked safe food is included
If no direct fit exists, verify an explicit fallback safe food is suggested
Verify the output never silently omits toddler compatibility

features.description: Planner computes overlap score for ingredients and meals
steps: Open ingredient or meal details
View overlap score, compatibility summary, and quick visual indicators on a meal card or details view.
Verify score reflects how many household members can eat it directly or with acceptable adaptation
Verify members with hard-no conflicts are clearly identified
Verify overlap score is used in meal ranking

features.description: User can generate a low-decision weekly plan from available meals and ingredients
steps: Open weekly planning
Verify the Home screen can link directly into weekly planning and the weekly planner uses a visual mini-calendar rather than a table.
Generate a five- or seven-day plan
Verify each day contains a base meal and per-person variants
Verify repeated ingredients are reused intentionally across the week
Verify the plan can be saved and re-opened

features.description: App produces one merged grocery list from the weekly plan
steps: Generate and save a weekly plan
Open the grocery list
Verify repeated ingredients are consolidated
Verify pantry or already-owned items can be excluded
Verify the list uses the shared styling system, remains easy to scan on mobile, and links back to meals using each ingredient where relevant.

features.description: Rescue mode generates the fastest acceptable meal using household staples first
steps: Open rescue mode
Verify rescue mode is reachable in one tap from Home, uses the shared styling system, and presents a simpler flow than the standard planner.
Choose a scenario such as low-energy, low-time, or everyone-melting-down
Generate a rescue meal
Verify the output prioritizes freezer or pantry staples when available
Verify the output still includes per-person assemblies and can be added to tonight or the week.

features.description: User can pin household-approved meals into a rotation
steps: Generate or open a meal that worked well
Pin it to the household rotation
Open the rotation view
Verify pinned meals are saved and reusable
Verify future planning can prioritize pinned meals

features.description: User can mark meals as success, partial success, or failure and capture quick notes
steps: Open a planned meal after serving
Record an outcome and optional notes
Save the result
Re-open meal history
Verify the outcome is persisted and visible

features.description: Planner learns from meal outcomes to rank reliable meals higher
steps: Record several successful and failed meal outcomes
Generate a new plan
Verify meals with stronger success history are ranked higher when constraints are similar
Verify hard-no or repeated-failure meals are deprioritized
Verify ranking remains explainable to the user

features.description: App loads quickly and supports same-session editing without noticeable lag for a typical household
steps: Launch the app with a representative fixture household
Navigate across setup, planning, and grocery screens
Edit member constraints and regenerate a plan
Verify interactions feel immediate for local MVP use
Verify no screen hangs or crashes during normal flows

features.description: All critical planning flows are covered by deterministic fixtures and end-to-end tests
steps: Run the automated test suite
Verify household setup flow is covered
Verify base meal planning flow is covered
Verify rescue mode is covered
Verify grocery list generation is covered

features.description: Planner explains why a meal was recommended and what the trade-offs are
steps: Generate a meal recommendation
Inspect the explanation panel
Verify the app states why the meal fits the household
Verify any extra prep or fallback requirements are surfaced
Verify the explanation is brief, understandable, and visible directly on meal cards or in one tap.

features.description: Planner allows quick editing of a single member without redoing the whole household setup
steps: Open an existing household
Edit one member's constraints
Save changes
Regenerate the meal plan
Verify only the relevant compatibility results change and the edit path avoids reopening the full household flow.

features.description: User can export or print the weekly plan and grocery list
steps: Open a saved weekly plan
Choose export or print
Verify the plan includes daily meals, per-person variants, and grocery list
Verify formatting is readable
Verify exported output matches the saved plan and grocery list formatting remains clean for printing.

features.description: Weekly planning screen displays meals in a visual mini-calendar layout
steps: Open weekly planning screen
Display current week as seven visual day cards in the Weekly Planner and as a compact strip when shown from Home.
Show the base meal assigned to each day
Allow empty days to display suggested meals
Verify the layout is readable on both desktop and mobile screens

features.description: User can drag meal cards into the weekly plan calendar
steps: Open weekly planner
Display suggested meal cards below the week grid
Drag a meal card onto a day slot
Verify the meal attaches to that day
Verify the weekly plan updates immediately and the interaction also supports tap-to-assign.

features.description: Meals are displayed as visually readable cards with compatibility indicators
steps: Open meal suggestions
Render each meal as a large card
Display meal name, prep time, effort level, and overlap score.
Display compatibility icons for ARFID adult, toddler, and baby
Verify cards remain readable, tappable, and draggable on mobile.

features.description: Planner shows visual overlap indicators and trade-offs for meals
steps: Open a meal card
Display compatibility indicators, trade-offs, and state chips such as high overlap or needs extra prep.
Highlight meals with high overlap
Flag meals requiring extra preparation
Verify indicators are clear and quickly understandable

features.description: Adopt a shared styling foundation using Tailwind CSS and reusable UI primitives
steps: Install and configure Tailwind CSS, or a directly comparable utility-first styling system, for the Vite + React app.
Create shared design tokens and base primitives for the page shell, cards, buttons, inputs, chips, lists, and empty states.
Add global typography, spacing, radius, and color rules that match the uiSpec designSystemDirection.
Refactor existing user-facing screens to use the shared primitives where practical without changing planner logic or storage behavior.
Adjust brittle UI tests that rely on old unstyled DOM structure so they assert accessible behavior instead.

features.description: Core screens apply the styling foundation for readable hierarchy, contrast, and fast scanning
steps: Audit currently implemented screens including Home, Planner, Weekly Planner, Household Setup, Member Profile, Ingredient Manager, and Base Meal Editor.
Replace remaining dense lists and form sections with card-based groupings that match the shared styling primitives.
Increase font sizes, spacing, and section hierarchy so users can scan meals and setup flows quickly.
Ensure contrast, focus states, and empty states meet accessibility expectations.
Verify the existing flows remain intact after the visual refactor and that users can scan meal information without reading long text.

features.description: Core screens are mobile-friendly with touch-first responsive layouts
steps: Review the styled core screens at narrow mobile widths before adding more UI features.
Convert wide or multi-column layouts into stacked, scrollable, or swipe-friendly mobile patterns where needed.
Ensure meal cards, weekly planner day cards, trays, and setup forms have large tap targets and readable spacing on small screens.
Verify the week view works as a horizontal scroll or stacked-card layout on mobile and that planner interactions still support tap-to-assign.
Adjust any existing layouts or components that became brittle during the styling refactor instead of layering one-off fixes.

features.description: Agent audits uiSpec alignment after the shared styling and mobile foundation are in place
steps: Read the uiSpec section in PRD.json
Map completed user-facing features to referenced screens using screenToFeatureMap and acceptanceRefs
Audit previously completed UI features against the referenced screen acceptance criteria now that the shared styling and mobile foundation exist
Record any remaining UI-specific follow-up work in agent-progress.md instead of silently leaving visual gaps
Verify PRD.json remains the single source of truth for both task order and screen-level requirements

features.description: Weekly planner displays preparation effort balance across the week
steps: Open weekly planner
Display effort labels such as low, medium, or higher effort
Show estimated prep time for each meal
Highlight days with multiple high-effort meals
Verify users can rebalance meals easily from the Weekly Planner.

features.description: Planner provides satisfying visual feedback when meals are added to the week
steps: Drag a meal onto a day
Display a brief confirmation animation, highlight, or satisfying state change.
Update overlap indicators and grocery list preview
Verify feedback is subtle and not distracting
Verify interactions feel responsive, satisfying, and subtle rather than distracting.

features.description: Planner supports multi-protein base meals under one shared meal structure
steps: Open the Base Meal Editor
Create a base meal with one shared structure and at least two protein options
Save the meal without duplicating it into separate recipe records
Open Meal Detail and verify the meal reads as one shared structure with multiple protein paths
Generate a plan and verify per-member variants can choose different protein options under the same base meal

features.description: Base meals can store recipe links and preparation notes
steps: Open the Base Meal Editor
Add one or more recipe links and freeform preparation notes to a base meal
Save the meal
Open Meal Detail and verify recipe links and notes are visible but do not dominate the page
Verify recipe links support multiple sources and notes support reminders such as Gousto versions or toddler adaptations

features.description: Planner learns household compatibility patterns from outcomes and quick edits
steps: Record several successful and failed meal outcomes plus one or more quick member edits
Generate a new recommendation or weekly plan
Verify the planner recognizes patterns such as sauce-separate preferences, repeated toddler safe foods, or preferred protein choices
Verify meals with stronger household fit are ranked higher while hard-no or repeated-failure paths are deprioritized
Verify ranking changes remain explainable to the user in concise language

features.description: Guided tour or interactive walkthrough explains how the app works end-to-end
steps: Implement a first-run guided tour or interactive walkthrough that introduces Home, Household Setup, Weekly Planner, Meal Cards, and Grocery List flows.
Show the tour only on first visit; persist completion state to browser storage (e.g. localStorage key such as onebase-tour-completed).
Allow users to skip or dismiss the tour at any step.
Verify the tour does not appear again after completion on subsequent visits.
Verify the tour aligns with the shared styling foundation and remains readable on mobile.

features.description: Warning confirmation before deleting a household or other entities
steps: Add a confirmation modal or warning dialog before deleting a household.
Apply the same confirmation pattern to all destructive delete flows: household members, ingredients, base meals, and other deletable entities.
Surface the entity name in the warning so the user knows exactly what will be removed.
Require explicit confirmation (e.g. tap Confirm or Delete) before proceeding; allow Cancel to abort.
Verify the warning uses the shared styling system and is accessible.

features.description: Revamp household navigation into a polished integrated app shell
steps: Replace the current plain text household link row on all household-scoped screens with a visually integrated navigation component that feels like part of the product, not a tacked-on list of links.
Use clear active state, spacing, grouping, and responsive behavior for Home, Weekly planner, Meal planner, Grocery list, Rescue mode, Ingredients, Base meals, and All households.
On desktop, render navigation as a cohesive header/tab bar or segmented nav block with active-page highlighting and consistent placement.
On mobile, render navigation as a wrapped pill row, bottom bar, or other touch-first pattern with active state and large tap targets; avoid cramped inline links.
Ensure the nav appears consistently across Home, Weekly Planner, Meal Planner, Grocery List, Rescue Mode, Ingredient Manager, Base Meal Manager, Meal Detail, and Household Setup.
Verify the active page is obvious at a glance and the screen no longer looks like a list of default links.
Verify Home remains the household hub and all back/up navigation still returns to /household/:id/home.

features.description: User can add photos to ingredients and base meals via URL or upload
steps: Add optional imageUrl field to ingredients and base meals in the data model.
In Ingredient Manager and Base Meal Editor, allow user to provide an image URL or upload a file (converted to data URL for local-first storage).
Display images in card layouts, list thumbnails, and hero-style on Meal Detail.
Verify images fit within the shared styling system (aspect ratio, borders, rounded corners) and remain readable on mobile.

features.description: Add ingredient or component inline or via modal with discoverable navigation
steps: Add an 'Add new ingredient' flow accessible from Base Meal Manager when adding a component (inline expandable form or modal).
Add Ingredients and Base meals links to navigation: either a submenu in HouseholdNav or flat links (Ingredients, Base meals) using shared styling.
Add links from Planner and Weekly Planner empty states to 'Add ingredients' and 'Add base meals' similar to Rescue mode's 'Add some base meals' link.
Verify users can create an ingredient without leaving the meal editor and that create flows are discoverable from the nav and empty states.

features.description: User can import and export data as JSON for backup and seeding
steps: Add export: user can download all households (or current household) as a JSON file.
Add import: user can upload JSON to replace or merge with existing data.
Add npm script (e.g. npm run seed or npm run db:import) that reads a JSON file, clears storage, and writes the data to the app's storage key.
Verify exported JSON matches the structure used by loadHouseholds/saveHouseholds and can be re-imported to restore or seed the app.

features.description: Database cleared and repopulated each build with McGeever family seed
steps: Add fixtures/households/H004-mcgeever.json with McGeever household: Aaron (adult), Rachel (adult), Indy (toddler), Órla (baby), Lex (dog/pet).
Extend MemberRole to include pet; exclude pet members from assembly variants, overlap scoring, and rescue logic.
Add seed script (e.g. npm run db:seed) that loads fixtures and writes to storage.

features.description: Clicking a household navigates to Edit setup; remove Household setup from nav
steps: Change HouseholdList: clicking household card/name goes to /household/:id (HouseholdSetup) instead of /household/:id/home.
Remove 'Household setup' link from HouseholdNav.
Keep 'Home' link in HouseholdNav so users can reach Home from within a household.
Remove or repurpose redundant Setup button on household card if it duplicates the main click target.
Verify clicking a household opens the edit/setup screen and users can reach Home via nav.

features.description: User can share a mobile-friendly screenshot of the weekly plan via text
steps: Add capability to capture the weekly planner view (or a dedicated share view) as an image.
Use navigator.share with the image when available, or fallback to download for manual share.
Ensure the captured view uses shared styling and is readable at mobile sizes when shared.
Verify the share flow works on mobile and desktop.

features.description: Ingredient Manager becomes a compact browse-first paginated list with modal editing and bulk selection
steps: Replace the current permanently expanded ingredient cards with a browse-first Ingredient Manager built for fast scanning.
Show search, category filter, tag filter, source filter, sort, and page-size controls in a sticky compact control bar above the list.
Render ingredients as compact rows or compact stacked list items that surface name, category, tags, freezer-friendly, baby-safe, source, and optional thumbnail at a glance.
Use traditional pagination (page numbers, prev/next) instead of infinite scroll or load-more for large ingredient libraries.
Open ingredient details in a modal or drawer for edit instead of keeping every ingredient expanded inline.
Keep destructive delete actions secondary and place them inside the modal/drawer or an overflow menu.
Support bulk selection via per-row checkboxes, select-all-on-page, select-all-filtered, and clear selection.
Ensure the page remains comfortable to use with 1000+ ingredients on mobile and desktop.
Verify the Ingredient Manager no longer feels like a giant form or a stack of bulky cards.

features.description: App ships with a seeded ingredient catalog and add-from-catalog flow
steps: Add a master ingredient catalog separate from household ingredients and seed it with common proteins, carbs, veg, fruit, dairy, snacks, freezer items, and pantry staples.
Make the default 'Add ingredient' flow search the catalog first instead of forcing manual entry.
Allow the user to add a catalog ingredient to the household with one action and optional pre-save edits.
Preserve manual ingredient creation for uncommon household items.
Verify catalog-backed ingredients work with the existing planner, grocery list, rescue mode, and base meal flows.

features.description: Household ingredients support catalog linkage, lightweight customization, and duplicate handling
steps: Store whether a household ingredient was created manually or linked from the master catalog.
Audit existing ingredient records for legacy casing, spacing, and trailing punctuation inconsistencies before merge operations.
Run a legacy normalization migration that lowercases names, trims whitespace, collapses duplicate internal spaces, and removes trailing punctuation where appropriate.
Warn when a near-duplicate ingredient already exists in the household list.
Allow the user to merge or cancel duplicate additions.
Detect duplicates using normalized lowercase names across legacy ingredients, manual entries, imported ingredients, and catalog-linked ingredients.
Merge legacy duplicates safely by keeping one surviving ingredient record, reassigning related references, and only then removing or archiving duplicates.
Update all related references (meal components, recipe-imported links, tags, catalog links, grocery/planner associations, and other ingredient relationships) so no orphaned ids remain.
Make the legacy migration idempotent where possible and avoid destructive data loss by preferring merge and reassignment over direct deletion.
Use normalized lowercase ingredient names as the duplicate-matching key across manual entries, imported ingredients, and catalog-linked ingredients.
Allow household-specific edits to tags, notes, image, freezer-friendly, and baby-safe flags without mutating the master catalog entry.
Display ingredient names in sentence case in the UI while keeping canonical storage lowercase.
Validate end-to-end that ingredient lists, meal associations, imports, and catalog links still work after migration and duplicate merges.
Verify previously saved manual ingredients continue to work without migration failures.

features.description: Recipe import parses ingredients and builds a reviewable base-meal draft
steps: Allow the user to paste recipe text or a recipe URL into a new import flow.
Parse ingredient lines into a review screen and attempt to match them against household ingredients and the master catalog.
Normalize parsed ingredient names before lookup using lowercase, trimmed whitespace, collapsed duplicate spaces, and trailing punctuation removal.
Let the user confirm matches, create missing ingredients, or ignore unmatched lines before saving.
When no match exists, create the ingredient with the normalized lowercase name.
Create a draft base meal with mapped components, attached recipe link, and optional notes instead of blindly auto-saving a finished meal.
Verify imported ingredients can be added to the household pantry during review and the result stays compatible with the local-first storage model.

features.description: Run legacy ingredient migration to normalize existing records and safely merge duplicates
steps: Audit existing stored household ingredients for casing, spacing, and trailing punctuation inconsistencies using the canonical normalization rules.

Implement and execute a migration over existing stored data that lowercases names, trims surrounding whitespace, collapses repeated internal spaces, and removes trailing punctuation where appropriate.
Detect duplicates after normalization and select one surviving ingredient record per normalized name.
Reassign all related references from duplicate ingredient ids to the surviving id across meal components, recipe-import mappings, tags, catalog links, grocery references, and planner associations.
Remove or archive duplicate ingredient records only after reassignment succeeds and preserve useful metadata from the most complete duplicate.
Ensure migration behavior is idempotent where possible and avoids destructive data loss.
Validate end-to-end that ingredient lists, meal associations, imports, and catalog links continue to work after migration and no orphaned references remain.
Verify ingredient names remain stored in lowercase while UI display remains sentence case.

features.description: App can import Paprika .paprikarecipes exports, preserve recipe provenance, and convert recipes into reviewable base-meal drafts
steps: Add a Paprika import flow that accepts a .paprikarecipes file upload from the Ingredient Manager or Base Meal Manager.
Parse the .paprikarecipes archive format and extract recipe records plus structured metadata without requiring manual paste or per-recipe URL entry.
Retain imported recipe provenance on drafts and saved meals, including source system, external recipe id when available, source URL, import timestamp, and optional sync timestamp.
Retain structured Paprika recipe metadata where available, including prep time, cook time, total time, difficulty, servings, categories, notes, and optional image/photo URL.
Map Paprika total time to BaseMeal estimatedTimeMinutes for planner compatibility while also preserving separate prep and cook time fields when available.
Define MealComponent explicitly in the PRD and implementation as a first-class meal-scoped object rather than an implicit array shape on BaseMeal.
Store component-level metadata for imported meals, including role, optional quantity and unit, original source line, match type, and optional confidence.
During import review, preserve each parsed ingredient line as an explicit mapping record instead of discarding the import decision after save.
Support imported ingredient match outcomes of use existing ingredient, create new ingredient, or ignore, and persist that result with the imported meal draft.
Allow the user to prepare Paprika intake in bulk, including import-all, import-selected, or import-by-category recipe selection before entering ingredient-resolution review.
Create reviewable draft base meals with mapped components, recipe links, retained Paprika metadata, and optional notes instead of blindly auto-saving finished meals.
Detect duplicate imported meals and duplicate ingredient matches using normalized lowercase rules and offer skip, merge, or keep-both review actions where appropriate.
Show imported recipe provenance and retained timing metadata on the saved base meal detail screen in a lightweight way that does not dominate the page.
Allow the user to reopen an imported meal later and understand which components came from which original recipe lines without re-parsing the archive.
Verify imported meals remain compatible with planner, grocery list, duplicate handling, images, and local-first storage after Paprika metadata and mappings are added.

features.description: Paprika import quality gates minimize manual cleanup for large imports through stronger parsing, normalization, and match confidence
steps: Treat import success as a product-quality target: large Paprika imports are not complete unless manual ingredient cleanup is substantially reduced versus per-line intervention.

Normalize parsed ingredient strings before matching using canonical lowercase rules plus punctuation cleanup, pluralization normalization, spacing collapse, common abbreviation normalization, and quantity/unit token cleanup.
Strip quantities and units before ingredient-name matching while preserving parsed quantity value and parsed unit for component data and import audit records.
Ignore non-ingredient lines during parsing and review candidate generation, including section headers, subheadings, notes, and lines such as 'ingredients for filling' and 'other ingredients'.
Detect and ignore instruction-like lines that are not ingredients, including symbol-prefixed notes, imperative cooking phrases, and long freeform instructional text.
Support robust quantity parsing for decimals, fractions, mixed fractions, unitless counts, parenthetical notes, preparation descriptors, and post-quantity 'of' phrasing cleanup.

Correctly parse ingredient names from lines such as '1 cup quinoa (any color), rinsed well', '1 pinch of salt', '1.5 cups of water', '1 lime, zested and squeezed', '1/2 cup grated Parmesan', and '4 cups low-sodium beef broth'.
Strip preparation descriptors like grated, shredded, chopped, diced, minced, sliced, crushed, zested, peeled, rinsed, and drained before canonical matching while preserving prep metadata.
Preserve compound ingredient names such as beef broth, chicken broth, olive oil, soy sauce, parmesan cheese, diced tomatoes, and cannellini beans during matching instead of collapsing to broad nouns.
Improve fuzzy and alias matching quality for common pantry items, spices, cheeses, herbs, sweeteners, and frequent household variants while preserving household-first matching priority.
Produce suggested matches with explicit confidence levels (for example exact/strong/low-confidence) so low-confidence matches can be reviewed before save.

Preserve raw ingredient lines, parsed quantity value, parsed quantity unit, cleaned ingredient name, prep notes, match type, confidence level, chosen action, and final matched ingredient id for later auditing and re-editing.
Prevent creating draft meals from the import batch until unresolved ingredient lines are either resolved to an explicit action or intentionally ignored.

Verify the improved flow still preserves MealComponent and ImportMapping-compatible metadata, respects duplicate handling and canonical lowercase storage, and remains compatible with planner, grocery list, and local-first storage.

features.description: Paprika bulk import resolution UX groups repeated unresolved ingredients and enables fast, safe exception-first review
steps: Provide a bulk ingredient-resolution table or list for Paprika imports with unresolved count visibility and grouped status counts for unresolved, ignored, matched, and low-confidence lines.
Group repeated unresolved lines by normalized ingredient name so users resolve one grouped issue instead of handling every duplicate line independently.
Support one-click apply-to-all behavior that applies a chosen resolution to all occurrences sharing the same normalized ingredient name across the selected import set.
Support line and group actions: match existing ingredient, add from catalog, create new ingredient, and ignore.
Support batch actions for repeated unresolved groups and for commonly repeated obvious outcomes without forcing per-line review.
Support review filters for unresolved, ignored, matched, and low-confidence, plus an exceptions-only view that hides already-safe lines.
Require low-confidence suggestions to remain visible in exceptions views until explicitly accepted, replaced, or ignored.
Show when mapping progress is saved, persist progress safely, and allow the user to leave and return without losing grouped resolutions or per-line overrides.
Apply duplicate-prevention safeguards: when a likely existing household or catalog match is available, do not silently create a new noisy duplicate ingredient entry.
Validate that final import save creates draft base meals only after grouped unresolved items are handled or intentionally ignored, while preserving provenance and import mapping auditability.

features.description: Base Meal Editor (S007) is refactored in place into a structure-first meal-building flow with compact component editing
steps: Refactor the existing Base Meal Editor screen in place so it reads as a meal-building tool instead of a long generic form, without creating a parallel editor.

Establish a clear section hierarchy in the editor flow: meal identity/header first, components/structure second, planning metadata third, and secondary reference content (recipe links, notes, image) last.
Render components as compact collapsed rows or cards by default, with concise summaries and expand-on-demand editing so repeated controls are not shown all at once.

Improve protein alternatives UX by clearly separating the default ingredient from alternatives, rendering alternatives as visible chips/cards, and providing an explicit add-alternative action with searchable/selectable picker behavior.
Keep inline ingredient creation available while editing components and ensure newly created ingredients are immediately selectable in component and alternative flows.
Move recipe links, notes, and image editing into lower-priority collapsible sections that remain editable and persistent but no longer dominate the top of the editor.
Make destructive actions calmer and secondary to save actions while preserving explicit delete confirmation behavior.
Clarify save behavior with a clear primary save action label and use sticky header/footer treatment (or equivalent) so long editor sessions remain easy to navigate on desktop and mobile.
Preserve routes, local-first storage behavior, planner compatibility, and existing saved meal data semantics throughout the refactor.

features.description: Create Household (S006) is polished in place into a compact, guided setup with clearer action hierarchy
steps: Refine the existing Create Household screen in place with clear hierarchy sections for header, household details, and members without changing routes or data model behavior.
Replace always-expanded member blocks with compact member rows/cards that surface name, role, and texture level at a glance, with inline expand/edit behavior.
Replace the oversized member empty state with a lighter, less dominant empty state and explicit call to action text such as 'Add your first member'.
Keep Add member visible and low-friction from the members section and near the final action area so adding members feels fast.
Make Create household the clear primary action while keeping Add member secondary and visually calmer.
De-emphasize remove-member controls while preserving explicit confirmation behavior before deletion.
Maintain responsive mobile usability and preserve existing local-first create/edit flows for household and member data.

features.description: Separate global navigation from section navigation for consistent wayfinding
steps: Use one consistent global navigation bar across household pages with only top-level pages: Home, Weekly planner, Meal planner, Grocery list, Rescue mode, and Meal history.
Move section-level links such as Households, Ingredients, and Base meals into a separate secondary navigation shown only on section pages.
Render section navigation below the page title or section header so users can distinguish app-wide movement from section-local movement.
Remove mixed navigation rows that combine top-level and section-level destinations.
Maintain consistent order, active-state styling, and spacing for global and section navigation patterns.
Preserve existing routes, page components, and storage/planner behavior while refactoring navigation structure.

features.description: Weekly Planner suggested meals show a small ranked subset by default with browse-all for large libraries
steps: Rank suggested meals by household overlap first, then outcome and learned pattern scores, ingredient reuse against the current week, pins, and prep/effort tie-breakers; deprioritize in-week repeats and failure-only history unless pinned or a clear favorite.
Show only a capped number of suggestions on the main planner (responsive: fewer on narrow viewports, more on desktop) with a horizontal scroll strip on small screens.
Provide Browse all meals with search, optional effort filter, and paginated meal cards so the main planner never renders the full library at once.
Preserve assign, pin, details, and drag/drop from suggested cards.
Add automated tests for ranking order, default cap, browse path, and pagination.

features.description: Meal Planner choose/build/use: selected meal panel with cooking-first how-to, component recipe refs, plan-time overrides, and primary actions
steps: Selecting a meal from the tray or browse opens a useful selected-meal state with shared structure, component groups, per-member assembly, trade-offs, and primary actions (Use tonight, Add to week, Edit meal).
Support optional ComponentRecipeRef on each meal component (internal base meal, imported recipe, URL, note) while keeping BaseMeal.recipeLinks backwards-compatible.
Show a scannable How to make tonight section grouped by component with default vs tonight-only overrides; do not overwrite base meal defaults unless the user explicitly saves defaults.
Persist plan-specific overrides on DayPlan; Use tonight assigns the meal to today in the latest weekly plan with overrides attached.
Base Meal Editor: compact attach/change/remove recipe flow searching household meals first.
Add automated tests for selection UX, how-to section, overrides, and multi-meal tray caps.

features.description: Weekly theme anchors: soft weekday themes, household editor, weak ranking boost, non-blocking UI
steps: Household can optionally define weekly anchors (weekday, label, tags, structure types, optional meal ids, enabled).
Weekly Planner shows theme context on day cards; suggested meals can show a subtle matches-theme signal when ranking uses that day’s anchor.
Theme match is a late tie-breaker in rankWeeklySuggestedMeals so poor-fit theme meals never beat clearly better household fits.
Meal Planner accepts optional ?day= for theme microcopy and ranking context.
Add tests for anchor persistence, ranking boost behavior, and non-blocking assignment.

features.description: First-class Recipe model: typed recipe categories, RecipeRef pointers on BaseMeal/Ingredient, explicit recipeId on ComponentRecipeRef, migration for existing data
steps: Add RecipeType union (whole-meal, component, sauce, sub-recipe, batch-prep) and optional recipeType, parentRecipeId, directions, tags fields on Recipe.
Add RecipeRef type (recipeId, label, role, notes) as a lightweight pointer to a Recipe library entry.
Add recipeRefs on BaseMeal for whole-meal/assembly/shortcut recipe references alongside existing recipeLinks.
Add recipeId on ComponentRecipeRef for explicit FK to Recipe, formalizing the F055 cooking-first flow.
Add defaultRecipeRefs on Ingredient as an optional fallback (not the primary resolution model).
Add v2 storage migration: backfill recipeRefs from sourceRecipeId, copy importedRecipeSourceId to recipeId on ComponentRecipeRef, infer recipeType for imported recipes, carry Paprika directions.
Update promoteRecipeToBaseMeal to populate recipeRefs on promoted meals.
Update Paprika import to set recipeType and directions on Recipe.
Add tests covering recipe persistence, backward compatibility, migration safety, component recipe refs, imported provenance, and planner flow.

features.description: Recipe attachment and suggestion UX: contextual recipe search, whole-meal recipe refs, alt-protein recipes, full cooking resolution chain, recipe cues on weekly planner, prep-ahead opportunities, and enhanced meal detail
steps: Extend ComponentRecipePicker to accept and search household.recipes with priority: household recipes first, imported recipes second, URL/note fallback last.
Add whole-meal RecipeRef attachment UI in BaseMealManager MealModal section 4 (attach/remove recipe refs on BaseMeal.recipeRefs).
Add alternative-protein recipe attachment in ComponentForm per alternative ingredient using alt: prefix convention.
Thread household.recipes through BaseMealManager, MealModal, ComponentForm, and ComponentRecipePicker.
Implement resolveFullCookingRef with full priority chain: session override, DayPlan override, component default, whole-meal recipe, ingredient default, prepNote/recipeLinks fallback.
Update Planner How to make tonight section: group by component role, show whole-meal recipe refs, display resolution source labels.
Add subtle recipe cue chips on WeeklyPlanner DayCard: recipe count, prep-ahead, batch-friendly indicators.
Implement findPrepAheadOpportunities to detect repeated ingredients across week days with batch-prep recipes, surface in Weekly Planner effort bar.
Enhance MealDetailContent to show whole-meal recipeRefs, component-level recipes, and alternative-protein recipe indicators.
Add subtle recipe count cue on MealCard stats line.
Recipe suggestions appear only at: component editing in Base Meal Editor, meal selection in Planner, assigned meal details in Weekly Planner, repeated-component prep-ahead detection.
Add tests covering resolution chain priority, recipe attachment flows, tonight override vs saved default, alt-protein handling, recipe counting, batch-prep detection, and prep-ahead opportunities.

features.description: Expanded seed content: broader ingredient catalog with aliases, example recipes, and example base meals demonstrating the planning + cooking model
steps: Expand MASTER_CATALOG with taco, pizza, pasta bake, rice bowl, sauce, and pantry-basic ingredients.
Add optional aliases field to CatalogIngredient for future matching and import quality; update searchCatalog to match aliases.
Add household-level ingredients to H001 seed data for all ingredients referenced by new recipes and meals.
Seed 10 example recipes (component, sauce, whole-meal types) covering taco, pizza, pasta bake, rice bowl, and rescue use cases.
Seed 5 example base meals (Taco night, Pizza night, Pasta bake, Rice bowl, Cheesy pasta rescue) with recipeRefs and component-level ComponentRecipeRefs wired to seeded recipes.
Include alternative protein options (black beans for taco, tofu for bowl) to demonstrate the one-base-meal-many-plates concept.
Include a rescue-eligible meal (Cheesy pasta) with pantry staples to demonstrate rescue mode.
Ensure all ingredient IDs, recipe IDs, and recipeRef wiring is internally consistent with no duplicates.
Verify seeded data works with planner overlap scoring, grocery list generation, rescue mode, meal detail, and cooking panel.

features.description: Paginated ingredient table with bulk selection and safe bulk delete for large ingredient libraries
steps: Replace infinite-scroll/load-more with traditional paginated data-table (page numbers, prev/next, first/last controls).
Add page-size control (25, 50, 100 items per page) in the control bar.
Add source filter (manual, catalog, imported) to the control bar alongside existing search, category, tag, and sort.
Make the control bar sticky so filters remain accessible while scrolling.
Add per-row selection checkboxes that do not trigger modal editing.
Add select-all-on-page header checkbox with indeterminate state.
Add bulk actions bar (visible when 1+ items selected) with select-all-filtered, clear selection, and delete selected.
Selection persists across page navigation and filter changes within the session.
Bulk delete opens a confirmation dialog showing selected count, sample ingredient names, and reference-safety analysis.
Scan baseMeals, recipes, weeklyPlans, and importMappings for ingredient ID references before allowing deletion.
If all selected ingredients are unreferenced, allow bulk delete with confirmation.
If some are referenced, offer to delete only the unreferenced subset with a clear explanation of which items are protected and why.
If all are referenced, block deletion and show only a close button.
After bulk delete, page and pagination recover gracefully (auto-navigate to valid page).
Desktop rows show checkbox, thumbnail, name, category, tags, source, and flags in aligned columns.
Mobile rows use a compact stacked layout with checkbox, name, and inline chips.
Result summary shows total items, filtered count, selected count, and current page/total pages.
Empty and no-results states render correctly after bulk operations.

fixtureHouseholds.name: Conflicting household baseline
fixtureHouseholds.description: One adult with strict preparation constraints, one picky toddler, one no-teeth weaning baby, and one adult with unrelated preferences.

fixtureHouseholds.name: Two adults plus toddler
fixtureHouseholds.description: Simpler fixture used to prove the planner still works when no baby adaptations are needed.

fixtureHouseholds.name: Single caregiver plus two children
fixtureHouseholds.description: Used to test rescue mode and low-time planning flows.

fixtureHouseholds.name: McGeever family
fixtureHouseholds.description: Aaron (adult), Rachel (adult), Indy (toddler), Órla (baby), Lex (dog). Primary seed data for build-time repopulation.

uiSpec.intent: Embedded screen-level UX requirements for agents that implement features sequentially from this PRD. Agents must consult the referenced screen definitions before marking related UX or functional features as passing.
productPrinciples: Reduce stress, not merely organize it.
Every core screen should help the user feel more capable within 5 seconds of opening it.
Prioritize one obvious action per screen.
Use card-first layouts, large typography, and strong visual hierarchy.
Make planning feel satisfying without streaks, guilt, or pressure.
Home is the household hub; all back/up navigation from child screens returns to Home, not to HouseholdSetup. Navigation links must feel integrated into the page layout, not strange or out of place.

screens.name: Home
screens.goal: Help the user decide tonight's meal quickly or jump into this week's plan. Home is the canonical household hub for navigation.
requiredElements: Headline such as 'What should we eat tonight?'
Mini weekly strip showing the current week
Recent wins or pinned meals row
Consistent nav section linking to Weekly planner, Grocery list, Rescue mode, Meal planner, Ingredients, Base meals, All households
acceptance: User can reach a tonight decision in two taps or fewer from the home screen
Home screen does not require scrolling to find the main action
At least one suggestion clearly explains why it fits the household
Child screens (Weekly planner, Meal planner, etc.) have a back/Home control that returns to this screen, not to HouseholdSetup

screens.name: Meal Cards
screens.goal: Present meals as easy-to-scan options with household fit context.
requiredElements: Compatibility chips for ARFID adult, toddler, baby, and other adult
Short reason such as 'sauce separate works' or 'toddler safe food included'
Quick actions: assign, save, open details
acceptance: User can understand the fit of a meal without opening details
Cards remain legible on small mobile screens
Cards support both tap-to-open and drag-to-assign behaviors

screens.name: Weekly Planner
screens.goal: Let the user build this week's dinners visually with a mini-calendar.
requiredElements: Seven day cards for the current week
Assigned meal or empty state per day
Suggested meal tray below the week showing only a small ranked subset by default (not the entire recipe library)
Header on suggested section with title, count summary such as top N of total, and Browse all meals entry point
Browse all meals path (modal or equivalent) with search and lightweight filtering; full library loaded in pages, not all cards on the main planner
Suggested ranking uses household fit first, then learned outcomes and patterns, ingredient reuse vs current plan, pins, and lower prep/effort as tie-breakers
Visible effort balance for the week
Share button to capture and share plan as image via text or native share
Back/Home control that navigates to Home (household hub), not to HouseholdSetup
Consistent nav section (integrated with page layout) linking to sibling screens, Ingredients, Base meals, and Home
interactionRules: Dragging a meal to a day should immediately assign it
Assigned days should animate subtly on drop
Users can tap a day to swap or clear a meal
Week view should work on mobile as a horizontal scroll or stacked card layout
Suggested meal strip on mobile should support horizontal scroll without rendering every meal in the library
acceptance: User can build a week plan without entering a table or form view
Plan changes update grocery preview immediately
The planner remains readable even with all seven days filled and with a large base meal library
Users can open the full meal library from the planner without the default view becoming an infinite wall of cards
'Back to household' or equivalent returns user to Home screen; navigation links feel integrated, not tacked on

screens.name: Meal Detail
screens.goal: Show one-base-many-plates detail without overwhelming the user.
requiredElements: Base meal hero card with optional image display
acceptance: Multi-protein meals read as one shared structure, not separate recipes
Recipe links are visible but do not dominate the page
Each household member's variant is reachable in one tap

screens.name: Rescue Mode
screens.goal: Produce the fastest acceptable meal when time, energy, or patience is low.
navigationRules: Back or 'Home' control must navigate to Home (/household/:id/home), not HouseholdSetup.
NavBar must be visually integrated and use the same placement/styling as other household screens.
requiredElements: Scenario picker such as low energy, low time, or everyone melting down
One-tap add to tonight or add to week
Back/Home control and consistent nav section that returns to Home
copyGuidelines: Treat rescue mode as normal and supported, not as failure
Prefer phrases like 'fastest acceptable dinner' or 'good for tired nights'
acceptance: User can get a usable dinner idea in under 30 seconds
Rescue mode favors household safe foods and staples first
The screen feels simpler than the standard planner

screens.name: Household Setup
screens.goal: Capture constraints quickly without feeling like admin.
requiredElements: Member cards for each household member
Nav section with links to Home, Ingredients, Base meals, and sibling screens; nav styled consistently (no separate Household setup link when this screen is the edit target)
acceptance: User can edit one member without reopening the full household flow
Inputs feel guided and forgiving
The screen avoids giant forms where possible
Navigation links to Home, Weekly planner, Meal planner, etc. feel integrated into the layout, not strange or out of place

screens.name: Base Meal Editor
screens.goal: Let the user define one flexible meal structure with variants, links, and notes.
requiredElements: Add new ingredient inline or via modal when adding component
acceptance: User can create a multi-protein meal in one editor flow
Notes support reminders such as 'Gousto version works well' or 'blend toddler sauce'
Recipe links support multiple sources without breaking the local-first flow

screens.name: Grocery List
screens.goal: Show one clean merged shopping list from the weekly plan.
navigationRules: Back or 'Home' control must navigate to Home (/household/:id/home), not HouseholdSetup.
NavBar must be visually integrated and consistent with other household screens.
requiredElements: Links back to meals using that ingredient
acceptance: List is easy to scan on mobile while shopping
Repeated ingredients are merged clearly
The list does not feel like a raw debug output

screens.name: First-Run Tour
screens.goal: Introduce new users to how the app works end-to-end without overwhelming them.
requiredElements: Step-through walkthrough of Home, Household Setup, Weekly Planner, Meal Cards, and Grocery List
Skip or dismiss control on each step
First-time-only display via browser storage (e.g. localStorage)
acceptance: Tour shows only on first visit and does not reappear after completion
User can exit the tour at any step without losing app state
Tour aligns with shared styling and remains readable on mobile

screens.name: Ingredient Manager
screens.goal: Let the user browse, add, edit, and bulk-manage household ingredients quickly without giant forms, supporting 1000+ ingredient libraries.
requiredElements: Integrated household navigation using the shared navigation pattern
Sticky compact control bar with search, category filter, tag filter, source filter, sort, and page-size controls
Primary actions: Add ingredient, Add from catalog
Compact ingredient rows with selection checkbox, name, category, tags, source, key flags, and optional thumbnail
Tap or click to open ingredient detail modal or drawer
Modal or drawer form for full editing
Traditional pagination controls (page numbers, prev/next, first/last) instead of infinite scroll
Bulk selection: per-row checkbox, select-all-on-page, select-all-filtered, clear selection
Bulk actions bar with bulk delete and clear selection
Bulk delete confirmation dialog with reference-safety checks
Result summary showing total items, filtered count, selected count, and current page
Clear empty state that offers catalog add and manual add
acceptance: User can scan and manage 1000+ ingredients without endless scrolling
Desktop view is table-like but still visually lightweight and readable rather than like a dense data grid
Mobile view uses compact stacked list items with large tap targets
Editing an ingredient takes one tap from the browse list
The primary screen stays browse-first instead of showing every ingredient as an expanded form
Bulk delete is safe: referenced ingredients are protected, only unreferenced items can be deleted
No orphaned ingredient references are created by bulk delete
Selection persists across page changes and filter changes within a session

microInteractions.name: Meal assigned
microInteractions.behavior: A day card briefly highlights and settles when a meal is added.
microInteractions.purpose: Makes planning feel satisfying without being noisy.

microInteractions.name: High-overlap indicator
microInteractions.behavior: Compatibility chips brighten subtly for meals with strong overlap.
microInteractions.purpose: Helps the user spot likely wins quickly.

microInteractions.name: Rescue confidence
microInteractions.behavior: Rescue suggestions display calm labels such as 'good for tired nights' or '12-minute save'.

microInteractions.name: Delete confirmation
microInteractions.behavior: Before any destructive delete (household, member, ingredient, base meal), show a confirmation modal with the entity name and require explicit confirmation.

agentExecutionRules: Before implementing a feature, read the feature object and any screen definitions listed in acceptanceRefs or screenToFeatureMap.
A feature that changes user-facing UI must not be marked passing until both the feature steps and the referenced uiSpec screen acceptance criteria are satisfied.
When multiple features affect the same screen, preserve existing requiredElements and acceptance criteria for that screen.
Prefer implementing the minimum vertical slice that makes the referenced screen usable end-to-end before moving to the next feature.
If a feature adds a new UI element, update the relevant screen implementation instead of creating an inconsistent parallel view.
When refactoring UI for styling or responsiveness, preserve existing routes, storage behavior, and planner logic, and replace brittle DOM-structure tests with accessible behavior-focused assertions.
